one_line_takeaway: By pairing a fine-tuned Llama2 code-completer with pruned symbolic-execution detection, SOChecker is the first tool to find DASP10 vulnerabilities in incomplete Stack Overflow smart-contract snippets, reaching 68.2% F1 end-to-end and beating GPT-4 (33.2%) and ten conventional detectors that cannot even process fragmentary code.
key_finding: SOChecker substantially outperforms both LLM-only and traditional-tool baselines on fragmented SO snippets. End-to-end (RQ3), it reaches a weighted-average F1 of 68.2% versus 20.9% (GPT-3.5) and 33.2% (GPT-4); the GPTs achieve high recall but very low precision. Its Vulnerability Detector alone (RQ2, on compilable completed contracts) hits 83.4% w.a. F1, beating all 10 SmartBugs tools (best baseline Maian at 22.2%). The Code Completer correctly completes 75.5% (846/897) of snippets at far lower cost ($1.32 for Ours+GPT vs $57.88 for GPT-4). Traditional tools are shown to be structurally unusable on raw snippets: they errored on 770 of 897 fragmentary snippets, and on the 127 complete ones Slither/Smartcheck found only 1/2 true positives vs SOChecker's 14.
core_contribution: SOChecker, presented as the first tool designed to detect vulnerabilities directly in the incomplete/fragmented Solidity smart-contract code snippets found in Stack Overflow (SO) answers. Because traditional detectors require fully compilable contracts, SOChecker first uses a fine-tuned Llama2-chat-13b 'Code Completer' to turn a snippet into a compilable contract, then runs a conventional symbolic-execution 'Vulnerability Detector' (Z3-based CFG pattern matching) that is pruned back to only the original snippet's code paths so LLM-introduced code does not cause false positives. The tool detects the nine top DASP10 vulnerability classes. A second, motivational contribution is a developer survey (74 valid responses) quantifying how often SO code is reused without security checks, plus a curated 897-snippet SO dataset (released with source at github.com/BugmakerCC/SOChecker).
threat_model: The paper's own security concern is a code-reuse / supply-chain-style risk: smart-contract developers copy vulnerable Solidity code from Q&A sites (Stack Overflow) into deployed contracts, where the embedded flaws (e.g., Reentrancy, Denial of Service, Access Control) become exploitable by on-chain attackers. The paper's survey grounds this: 88.4% of respondents have reused SO code, but only 16.4% performed comprehensive security audits before doing so, and 86.4% can guarantee only 'basic' or lower security. SOChecker is a defensive detection tool, not an attack; the adversary is a malicious contract caller exploiting the reused vulnerable snippet (illustrated by a DoS-in-a-payout-loop motivating example from a real accepted SO answer).
limitations: Scope is deliberately limited to the nine DASP10 vulnerability classes and 'simple' SO snippets; the authors explicitly exclude complex modern vulnerabilities like price-manipulation attacks. Recall is the main weakness: SOChecker's overall recall is 55.2% (vs precision 92.0%), because the LLM completer often silently PATCHES the original vulnerability or alters snippet semantics during completion, which the pruning algorithm cannot recover — a fundamental tension in the completion-then-detect design. Pruning also fails when a snippet has too little info to match AST nodes or when the LLM renames/deletes original code. The detector still emits false alarms from Z3 timeouts on loop/factorial path conditions and from compiler-version differences (e.g., Solidity 0.8.x default overflow checks not modeled). Threats-to-validity: SO posts were gathered only via 5 tags (Solidity/Ethereum/ERC20/ERC721/Contract), possibly missing untagged discussions; and the survey's real-world-usage claim is not verified by tracing the code on-chain. The dataset had no Front-Running or Short-Address-Attack instances, so those classes are untested in RQ2/RQ3.

datasets_used: 897 curated Solidity code snippets from smart-contract-related Stack Overflow posts (filtered from 4,952 posts made after Oct 2021 to avoid GPT training-data leakage) — the main evaluation set
345,058 open-source Ethereum contracts from smart-contract-sanctuary (up to Mar 2023); top 1,000 by transaction volume (avg 711.03 lines) used as LoRA fine-tuning data
324 contracts sampled from the smartbugs-wild dataset (47,398 contracts) used for the llama2 vs Codellama model-selection comparison
Survey of smart-contract developers: 1,416 practitioners contacted (emails from GitHub contributors), 74 valid responses from 19 countries (5 excluded), avg experience 2.80 years
quantitative_results: End-to-end (RQ3) weighted-avg F1 on 897 SO snippets: SOChecker 68.2% (P 92.0% / R 55.2%) vs GPT-4 33.2% (P 23.1% / R 73.6%) vs GPT-3.5 20.9% (P 13.4% / R 57.5%)
Vulnerability Detector (RQ2) weighted-avg F1 83.4% on 846 completed contracts; per-class F1: RE 66.7, AC 80.0, AI 84.2, URV 75.0, DoS 66.7, BR 76.9, TM 95.2
Best traditional-tool w.a. F1 in RQ2: Maian 22.2%, Mythril 12.3%, Smartcheck 7.1%, Slither 6.6%; Conkas/Osiris/Oyente/Securify 0%; Honeybadger and Manticore found no positives
Code completion (RQ1) correct snippets: Ours+GPT 846/897 (75.5%); compilable count 889. Per-model #compilable/#correct: Base 537/442, GPT-3.5-turbo 774/669, GPT-4 736/649, Ours 795/677
Completion cost/time: Ours+GPT $1.32 in 7.1h vs GPT-4 $57.88 in 11.2h vs GPT-3.5 $8.27 in 7.8h (local Ours/Base free)
Model selection: fine-tuned llama2-chat-13b produced 141 compilable contracts vs Codellama-instruct-13b's 48 on the 324-contract test
On raw snippets, traditional tools errored on 770/897; of 127 complete contracts (18 vulns) SOChecker found 14 TPs vs Slither 1, Smartcheck 2

Survey: 88.4% reused SO code but only 16.4% did comprehensive security audits; 86.4% guarantee only basic-or-lower security, 13.6% advanced; 94.6% had accessed SO; only 20.0% ever used detection tools on SO code, 44.9% citing 'lack of support for direct code analysis on SO'
baselines_compared: GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4 (used both as completion baselines and as direct vulnerability-detection baselines)
10 SmartBugs SOTA smart-contract detectors: Conkas, Maian, Mythril, Osiris, Oyente, Securify, Slither, Smartcheck, Honeybadger, Manticore
